column2: The Los Angeles Harbor Deepening Project proposes dredging approximately 14.7 million cubic yards of material to deepen the harbor's entrance, main channels, and turning basins to -45 feet MLLW (and -51 feet in some areas) to accommodate larger, deeper-draft ships. Most of the dredged material would be used to build a 190-acre landfill behind a rock dike on the south side of Terminal Island, along with an adjacent shallow-water habitat area, relocation of the Terminal Island Treatment Plant outfall, and utility relocations. Construction was estimated to take about 30 months at a first cost of…
column7: Core project description figures (14.7M CY, 190 acres, $59.1M, 30 months, -45/-51ft depths, 100-200 workers) are well-supported by the cited pages. However, several specific figures in the affected_c…

column2: The project is located in the Port of Los Angeles and affects the nearby communities of San Pedro, Wilmington, and Terminal Island, with about 32,746 residents living within 1,000 yards of the harbor. Port-related commerce supports roughly 14,500 direct jobs and $2.56 billion in regional payrolls, and construction would create 100-200 temporary jobs. Other affected groups include commercial shipping interests, port tenants and workers, recreational boaters facing a 6,000-berth shortage in Los Angeles County, sport and commercial fishermen, and nearby schools, recreational facilities, and the …

Alternatives considered included no action, ocean disposal of dredged material, upland/land disposal, a reduced-fill 'environmental quality' plan (about 103 acres), and variations in landfill size and location, with the recommended plan selected for its higher economic benefits. The original landfill was reduced from 282 acres to 190 acres in response to wildlife agency concerns, and the Reeves Field/Seaplane Anchorage area was preserved rather than filled after objections from boating interests. Dredging method alternatives ranged from one large dredge to multiple smaller dredges, with hydra…

The project would permanently destroy about 190 acres of soft-bottom benthic and shallow-water habitat, eliminate roughly 150 metric tons of benthic biomass and several tons of fish, and remove about 40 percent of feeding habitat for the endangered California least tern colony on Terminal Island. Short-term impacts include turbidity, reduced dissolved oxygen, possible release of contaminants from sediments, construction noise, and air emissions of hydrocarbons, NOx, SOx, CO, and particulates from dredges, tugboats, and the Catalina quarry. Beneficial effects include long-term water-quality im…

The Draft EIS Supplement/EIR was distributed to about 400 individuals and agencies, and a December 1979 public hearing in San Pedro drew both supporters (stevedoring and shipping interests, the Maritime Administration) and critics. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service initially issued a jeopardy opinion and opposed the large fill, NMFS sought stronger mitigation, and California agencies including Fish and Game, the Coastal Commission, and the Department of Navigation and Ocean Development requested more analysis or opposed filling Reeves Field, which was ultimately preserved for small-craft boa…
